categories.name: Prompt Injection
categories.description: Manipulating LLMs through crafted inputs that cause the model to act on the attacker's instructions, bypassing safety measures or intended behavior.

categories.name: Sensitive Information Disclosure
categories.description: LLM inadvertently revealing confidential data through responses, including PII, credentials, proprietary information, or system prompt details.

categories.name: Supply Chain Vulnerabilities
categories.description: Risks from third-party components including pre-trained models, training data, plugins, and MCP servers that may contain vulnerabilities or malicious code.

categories.name: Data and Model Poisoning
categories.description: Manipulation of training data or model weights to introduce backdoors, biases, or vulnerabilities that affect model behavior.

categories.name: Improper Output Handling
categories.description: Failure to validate, sanitize, or handle LLM outputs before passing them to downstream systems, leading to XSS, SSRF, RCE, or privilege escalation.

categories.name: Excessive Agency
categories.description: Granting LLM agents too many capabilities, permissions, or autonomy, allowing them to take unintended harmful actions.

categories.name: System Prompt Leakage
categories.description: Extraction of system prompts that contain business logic, security rules, or proprietary information through adversarial queries.

categories.name: Vector and Embedding Weaknesses
categories.description: Vulnerabilities in vector databases and embedding pipelines including unauthorized access, cross-tenant leakage, and model inversion attacks.

categories.name: Misinformation
categories.description: LLM generating false or misleading content that is presented as factual, particularly dangerous in high-stakes domains like healthcare, legal, or finance.

categories.name: Unbounded Consumption
categories.description: Excessive resource consumption through uncontrolled LLM API usage, leading to denial of service or denial of wallet attacks.
